title: Heuristics: Manhattan vs Euclidean Distance
subtitle: A heuristic is a guess about remaining cost; the right guess depends on how movement is allowed.
summary: The path finder project supports taxicab and Euclidean heuristics, making this a natural comparison page.

artifact.title: Heuristic pressure changes the frontier
artifact.description: Toggle the demo and watch how the path frontier changes when the estimated distance changes.

contentBlocks.content:
Grid streetsManhattan distance matches movement where diagonal steps are not legal.
Open planeEuclidean distance matches straight-line movement.
GuaranteeThe heuristic should not overestimate if shortest-path optimality matters.

sections.heading: What the Heuristic Is Doing
body: The heuristic does not move the agent. It ranks candidates by giving the algorithm a directional estimate.
A weak heuristic expands too much. An overconfident heuristic can become greedy and wrong.

sections.heading: Choosing the Distance
body: Manhattan distance is right for four-neighbor grids. Euclidean distance is natural when diagonal or continuous movement is allowed.

pitfalls: Using Euclidean distance on a four-direction grid without checking admissibility.
Assuming a more complex heuristic is automatically better.
Forgetting that terrain costs can make geometric distance misleading.

quiz.question: Which heuristic fits four-direction grid movement best?
quiz.explanation: Manhattan distance counts horizontal and vertical steps, matching four-direction movement.
